attributes.stem:
A 79-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-month history of faecal incontinence. He reports three to four episodes per week of involuntary stool leakage, both during the day and at night. He underwent surgical repair of a rectal prolapse six months ago. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and hypercholesterolaemia. His medications include metformin, amlodipine, and atorvastatin.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals a soft, non-tender abdomen with normal bowel sounds. Digital rectal examination reveals normal anal tone without masses or tenderness. The surgical site appears well-healed without evidence of infection or recurrent prolapse.

He has trialled stool bulking agents, pelvic floor exercises and bowel training for four months with only minimal improvement.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with persistent faecal incontinence following rectal prolapse repair. Given the temporal relationship to surgery and inadequate response to conservative measures, surgical review is essential to exclude post-operative complications or recurrence.

Surgical referral is the most appropriate next step to evaluate for potential surgical complications or anatomical issues that may have developed post-operatively. While conservative measures can continue, specialist assessment is warranted given the onset following surgery and poor response to initial management.

Continue conservative management alone would be inappropriate given the post-surgical context and poor response after four months of therapy. While conservative measures should continue, specialist assessment is needed in parallel.

Loperamide may help reduce stool frequency but does not address the underlying cause. Starting new medications without surgical assessment could mask important symptoms or complications.

Colonoscopy is not indicated as the primary investigation as it would not evaluate post-surgical complications or sphincter function. Any requirement for endoscopy should be determined after surgical assessment.

Biofeedback therapy may be helpful but should be initiated after surgical assessment has excluded complications or the need for revision surgery. It would typically be recommended as part of a comprehensive management plan developed in conjunction with the surgical team.

The post-surgical timing of symptom onset raises concern for potential complications such as altered anatomy, sphincter injury, or recurrent prolapse that may not be apparent on basic examination. While conservative measures can continue, surgical evaluation is essential to guide appropriate management and exclude the need for revision surgery.
learningPoints: Surgical referral is indicated for faecal incontinence following rectal prolapse repair to exclude surgical complications.
Conservative measures including pelvic floor exercises and bowel training should be continued alongside specialist assessment.
Diabetes can contribute to faecal incontinence through autonomic neuropathy affecting anal sphincter function.
references: Assmann SL, Keszthelyi D, Kleijnen J, Anastasiou F, Bradshaw E, Brannigan AE, Carrington EV, Chiarioni G, Ebben LD, Gladman MA, Maeda Y. Guideline for the diagnosis and treatment of Faecal Incontinence—A UEG/ESCP/ESNM/ESPCG collaboration. United European Gastroenterology Journal. 2022 Apr;10(3):251-86. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1002/ueg2.12213

Sbeit W, Khoury T, Mari A. Diagnostic approach to faecal incontinence: What test and when to perform? World J Gastroenterol. 2021 Apr 21;27(15):1553-1562. doi: 10.3748/wjg.v27.i15.1553. PMID: 33958842; PMCID: PMC8058654. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8058654/
